note: None of these questions have been added to course.json. See the companion promotion-review document for ready/pending/excluded categorization. This pack was expanded on 2026-07-20 from 15 to 44 candidates in a second question-mining pass.

questions.prompt: How much food energy do a liferaft's emergency provisions supply per person, according to the ration standard?

choices.text: 10,000 kilojoules (about 2,400 calories) per person
1,000 kilojoules (about 240 calories) per person
choices.misconception: Understates the figure by a factor of 10 -- nowhere near enough to sustain a survivor.

choices.text: 20,000 kilojoules (about 4,800 calories) per person
choices.misconception: Overstates the figure -- roughly double the actual ration standard.

choices.text: No fixed figure -- rations vary entirely by manufacturer
choices.misconception: Incorrectly assumes there's no standard figure at all.

hints: This figure is the same one already taught for general survival-craft rations in Chapter 4.
Think in round numbers: ten thousand of one unit, roughly twenty-four hundred of the other.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's provisions supply 1 unit per person, providing roughly 10,000 kilojoules (about 2,400 calories) -- the same figure already confirmed by Chapter 4's manual-sourced Food content, giving this fact genuine cross-chapter, multi-source support.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck (vinci-2026-07-20-chapter-3-class-notes-source-intake.md, slide 6), cross-confirmed by the already-live Chapter 4 manual-sourced Food content (q-ch4man-no-food-without-water and the Chapter 4 Learn Mode Food module, both citing the same 10,000 kJ/2,400 cal figure).
questions.uncertaintyNote: The class deck also cites 1.5 L total water capacity and a 3-day survival note without food/water in the first 24 hours -- those two figures are class-note-only and not included in this question.
questions.learnModeMapping: Liferafts (topic 0) / cross-reference to Chapter 4 Food module

questions.prompt: What must a folding knife carried in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft be?

choices.text: A jackknife approved by the Commandant under approval series 160.043
Any locking-blade knife, with no specific approval requirement
choices.misconception: Contradicts the CFR requirement for a specific Commandant-approved jackknife type.

choices.text: A fixed-blade knife only -- folding knives are prohibited
choices.misconception: Reverses the requirement -- the regulation specifically calls for a jackknife (folding), not a fixed blade.

choices.text: The same floating sheath knife used elsewhere in the raft
choices.misconception: Conflates two separate, distinct required items -- the jackknife and the floating sheath knife are not the same tool.

hints: This is a specific Coast Guard approval-series number, not a general description.
Don't confuse this with the raft's other, separately-required floating knife.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(b) requires each folding knife in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft to be a jackknife approved by the Commandant under approval series 160.043 -- a distinct requirement from the raft's separately-required floating sheath knife.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slide 4 (CFR excerpt) and slide 9 (equipment bag list)
questions.sourceReference: 46 CFR 160.151-21(b), extracted directly from Document(6).PDF.pdf (public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-sea-trials/), confirmed in the Chapter 3 class-notes deck slide 4 (which itself reproduces this CFR text).
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- this is a direct CFR citation.

questions.prompt: What safety requirement applies to a liferaft's tin-opener?

choices.text: Each sharp part of the tin-opener must have a guard
It must be stored separately from all other equipment for safety
choices.misconception: Invents a storage requirement the regulation doesn't state.

choices.text: It must be blunt enough not to require a guard
choices.misconception: Contradicts the actual requirement -- a functional tin-opener needs a guard on its sharp parts, not a dulled edge.

choices.text: It must be the same floating design as the sheath knife
choices.misconception: Invents a floating requirement that doesn't apply to the tin-opener.

hints: Think about what would make a sharp tool in a crowded, moving liferaft safer to have aboard.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(g) requires each sharp part of a liferaft's tin-opener to have a guard -- a straightforward safety requirement for a sharp tool stored among survivors in a confined space.
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- this is a direct CFR citation not otherwise referenced by name in the class deck's own slide text (the deck's equipment lists don't call out the tin-opener specifically).

questions.prompt: What type of whistle is required in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft?

choices.text: A ball-type or multi-tone whistle of corrosion-resistant construction
Any standard plastic whistle, with no construction requirement
choices.misconception: Ignores the specific corrosion-resistance and type requirements the regulation states.

choices.text: A single-tone whistle only
choices.misconception: Too narrow -- ball-type or multi-tone are both acceptable, not a single specified tone.

choices.text: An electronic whistle powered by the water-activated battery
choices.misconception: Invents a powered-device requirement; the required whistle is a simple mechanical device, not electronic.

hints: Two whistle designs are both acceptable, as long as the material meets one standard.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(i) requires the whistle to be a ball-type or multi-tone whistle of corrosion-resistant construction -- material durability matters as much as the type of whistle.
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- direct CFR citation. Note this is a different whistle from the personal whistle already tested elsewhere in this course (e.g., on a PFD) -- this one is specifically a liferaft-kit item.

questions.prompt: What construction requirement applies to a liferaft's boarding ladder?

choices.text: The steps must provide a suitable foothold
It must be made entirely of rigid metal
choices.misconception: Invents a material requirement the regulation doesn't specify -- the requirement is functional (foothold), not about a specific material.

choices.text: It must be retractable into the raft's floor when not in use
choices.misconception: Invents a mechanical feature not required by the regulation.

choices.text: It must be usable only when the raft is fully inflated
choices.misconception: Invents a usage restriction the regulation doesn't state.

hints: The requirement is about function -- can your foot actually get purchase on the step -- not about material or mechanism.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-17(n) requires a liferaft's boarding ladder steps to provide a suitable foothold -- a functional requirement, since a survivor boarding from the water, possibly exhausted or in an immersion suit, needs a step that actually holds a foot securely.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 33-34 (boarding procedure: 'place your feet on the boarding ladder')
questions.sourceReference: 46 CFR 160.151-17(n), extracted directly from Document(6).PDF.pdf. Boarding procedure context confirmed by the Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 33-34.
questions.uncertaintyNote: The CFR text alone doesn't describe a boarding *procedure* -- the class deck supplies that context (feet on the ladder, grab internal lifelines, pull yourself in headfirst), which is class-note-supported only and not part of this specific CFR-sourced question.

questions.prompt: What do a liferaft's maintenance instructions require, at minimum?

choices.text: A checklist for monthly external visual inspections, an explanation of periodic servicing by an approved facility, and a log of inspections and maintenance
A single annual inspection with no other recordkeeping
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement -- monthly external checks and a maintenance log are both required in addition to periodic full servicing.

choices.text: Servicing only after the raft has actually been deployed and used
choices.misconception: Contradicts the requirement for regular, scheduled servicing regardless of whether the raft has been used.

choices.text: No formal maintenance schedule -- upkeep is left to the vessel operator's discretion
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific, mandatory maintenance-instruction content the regulation requires.

hints: Three separate things are required: something monthly, something periodic, and a record of both.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-61 requires a liferaft's maintenance instructions to include a checklist for monthly external visual inspections of the packed liferaft, an explanation of periodic servicing requirements at an approved servicing facility, and a log for maintaining inspection and maintenance records.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slide 19 (liferafts inspected annually, must not exceed 17 months from last inspection, at a Coast Guard-approved facility)
questions.sourceReference: 46 CFR 160.151-61, extracted directly from Document(6).PDF.pdf. The class deck's slide 19 gives a complementary but distinct figure (the 17-month maximum interval between full inspections) that is not part of this specific CFR-sourced question.
questions.uncertaintyNote: The class deck's '17 months' interval figure is a real, useful complementary fact but is class-note-only (not found in the extracted CFR text) -- drafted as a separate candidate below, not merged into this question.
questions.learnModeMapping: Liferaft Markings (topic 4) -- would also support a new Maintenance topic if one is added

questions.prompt: What is the minimum length of a liferaft's operating cord (painter)?

choices.text: At least 100 feet
At least 60 feet (10 fathoms)
choices.misconception: This is the figure associated with a lifeboat's heaving line in a separate regulation, not a liferaft's operating cord.

choices.text: At least 250 feet
choices.misconception: Overstates the figure well beyond what's specified.

choices.text: No minimum length is specified
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific minimum-length figure the class-notes deck states.

hints: This is the same line labeled 'Operating Cord (Painter)' on the liferaft parts diagram -- not the separately-labeled 'Heaving Line.'
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states the operating cord (painter) has a length of at least 100 ft, and independently labels a liferaft container's coiled line as '100' Sea Painter/Operating Cord' on its own equipment diagram -- three independent mentions within the same deck, all pointing to the painter, not the heaving line.
questions.uncertaintyNote: PENDING RECONCILIATION, not ready for promotion as-is. The already-live q-quiz1a-heaving-line-length tests this same '100 feet' figure but attributes it to the heaving line, not the painter/operating cord -- see the reconciliation report's dedicated section. Promoting this question before that conflict is resolved would put two contradictory 100-foot facts live at once. This candidate should only be promoted alongside a correction (or retirement) of q-quiz1a-heaving-line-length, not on its own.

questions.prompt: At approximately what depth does a liferaft's hydrostatic release activate if the ship sinks before manual launch?

choices.text: 5 to 15 feet
50 to 100 feet
choices.misconception: Overstates the trigger depth well beyond what the class-notes deck specifies -- a release that deep would risk the raft being trapped too long.

choices.text: At the surface, before any submersion occurs
choices.misconception: Contradicts the requirement for actual submersion pressure to trigger a hydrostatic release.

choices.text: Only once the ship has fully sunk to the seabed
choices.misconception: Would defeat the purpose of an early-release safety mechanism meant to free the raft well before the ship is fully lost.

hints: The figure is stated as a range, not a single exact number.
It's shallow enough that the container can still float free and reach the surface quickly.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states the hydrostatic release is triggered by water pressure once the ship sinks 5 to 15 feet deep, at which point the container floats free toward the surface -- confirmed both in the deck's own equipment diagram (labeled '5-15\'') and its text description of automatic launching.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Not found in the CFR text extracted so far in this project (the hydrostatic release's own approval standard, 46 CFR Subpart 160.062, has not been extracted). Awaiting manual or further CFR confirmation before promotion to a higher tier.

questions.prompt: A liferaft that may have to be moved by hand for launching is subject to what weight limit?

choices.text: Cannot exceed about 400 lbs (185 kg)
Cannot exceed 100 lbs
There is no weight limit under any circumstances
Cannot exceed 1,000 lbs

hints: A liferaft launched directly from its stowed position (no manual carrying) has no such limit -- this figure applies only when it must be moved.
questions.explanation: A liferaft that may have to be moved for launching cannot exceed about 400 lbs (185 kg / 407.8 lb precisely) -- but a liferaft launched directly from its stowed position via an inclined/hand-tilted rack, or served by a davit, has no such weight limit.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck (vinci-2026-07-20-chapter-3-class-notes-source-intake.md, slides 2 and 19 -- the same figure stated twice within the deck, rounded on slide 2 and precise on slide 19).
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only (internally self-consistent across two slides in the same deck, but not yet cross-confirmed by an independent source). Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, what is the minimum liferaft capacity?

choices.text: At least 6 persons
At least 25 persons
At least 2 persons
No minimum capacity is specified

hints: This is a floor, not a typical size -- most liferafts shown in this course's material carry well more than this minimum.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states a liferaft must have a capacity of at least 6 persons, with capacity marked on both the container and the raft itself, alongside the manufacturer's name.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: How long do a liferaft's canopy lights operate once activated, and from how far away can they be seen?

choices.text: 12 hours, visible from about 2 miles away
48 hours, visible from about 10 miles away
1 hour, visible from about half a mile away
12 hours, but with no stated visibility range

hints: Two lights are installed on the canopy and activate automatically at inflation -- no manual switch needed.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states two canopy lights are automatically activated at inflation, operate for 12 hours, and can be seen from about 2 miles away.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Don't confuse with the EPIRB's 48-hour minimum transmit time (a different device) already taught elsewhere in this course.

questions.prompt: What is true about a liferaft's pelican hook/plunger pin, used to manually trip the hydrostatic release?

choices.text: It can be released while under strain
It can only be released once all tension on the tie-down strap has been removed
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual design intent -- the release is specifically built to work under strain, since a real emergency won't always allow slack tension first.

choices.text: It requires a special tool to release
choices.misconception: Invents a tool requirement the class-notes deck doesn't state.

choices.text: It is only used for automatic, not manual, launching
choices.misconception: Contradicts its actual role -- it's specifically the manual-launch trip mechanism.

hints: Think about why this matters in a real emergency -- you may not have the luxury of slack tension when you need to trip the release.
questions.explanation: To manually launch a liferaft, you trip the pelican hook/plunger pin on the hydrostatic release to release the tie-down strap -- and the class-notes deck specifically notes this can be done while the hardware is under strain, an important design feature for a real emergency.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: When two rescuers are helping an injured person board a liferaft from the water, what are the first two steps?

choices.text: Place their outboard knees on top of the buoyancy tubes, then turn the injured person with their back toward the rescuers
Immediately pull the injured person straight up and over the buoyancy tubes by the arms
choices.misconception: Skips the positioning steps that make the technique work, risking injury to both the rescuers and the survivor.

choices.text: Have the injured person climb the boarding ladder unassisted while rescuers watch
choices.misconception: Contradicts the entire premise of an assisted-boarding technique for someone who cannot help themselves.

choices.text: Deflate part of the raft to lower the freeboard before attempting to board them
choices.misconception: Invents a step not part of the described technique, and would compromise the raft's buoyancy unnecessarily.

hints: The rescuers position themselves on the raft's structure first, before touching the injured person.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck describes a 6-step technique for two rescuers assisting an injured person aboard: (1) place outboard knees on top of the buoyancy tubes, (2) turn the injured person with their back toward the rescuers, (3) grab their lifejacket with inboard hands, (4) grab their upper arm with outboard hands, (5) push them slightly down in the water and use buoyancy to spring them up and over into the raft back-first, (6) the rescuers fall to either side as the injured person lands between them.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only, but highly specific and procedural -- a strong candidate for Learn Mode's currently-empty Assisting Injured People topic even before manual confirmation exists.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, which of these is explicitly NOT recommended as a way to board a liferaft?

choices.text: Jumping into the canopy opening from 4 feet or less
Boarding at the embarkation deck from a davit-launched raft
Climbing down the Jacob's ladder
Boarding from the sea using the boarding ladder

hints: Three of the four methods are simply listed as ways to board; one is explicitly flagged with a warning.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck lists four ways a liferaft may be boarded -- at the embarkation deck (davit-launched raft), by climbing down the Jacob's ladder, by jumping into the canopy opening from 4 feet or less, or from the sea with the boarding ladder -- and explicitly marks jumping into the canopy opening as 'NOT RECOMMENDED,' separately warning that jumping onto the canopy top itself can injure people inside or be injured by stowed equipment.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. This is a strong candidate for completing Learn Mode's currently-empty Boarding the Liferaft topic.

questions.prompt: When boarding a liferaft from the sea, what should you grab to steady yourself, and what should you specifically avoid grabbing?

choices.text: Grab the internal lifelines; avoid grabbing the canopy, since it tears easily
Grab the canopy for leverage; avoid the internal lifelines, which aren't rated for that load
choices.misconception: Reverses the guidance -- the canopy is specifically the thing to avoid grabbing, since it will tear.

choices.text: Grab the towing bridle; avoid the boarding ladder entirely
choices.misconception: Contradicts the described technique, which specifically has you place your feet on the boarding ladder first.

choices.text: Grab the external lifelines only; internal lifelines are for occupants already aboard
choices.misconception: Misapplies the internal/external lifeline distinction -- a swimmer actively boarding reaches for the internal lifeline once at the entrance.

hints: One structure is built to bear a person's weight; the other is thin fabric that isn't.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck describes boarding from the sea: place your feet on the boarding ladder, reach inside and grab the internal lifelines (which help steady you in rough water), then pull yourself headfirst into the raft -- but do not grab the canopy, since it will tear easily.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Complements the already-live q-quizr1-internal-vs-external-lifelines, which this question does not duplicate (that question tests the internal/external distinction in the abstract; this one tests the specific boarding technique).

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, which of the following is NOT one of the three liferaft types listed?

choices.text: Semi-rigid
Inflatable
Rigid liferaft
Davit-launched

hints: Three types are listed; one of the four answer choices is invented.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck lists three liferaft types: Inflatable, Rigid Liferaft, and Davit-launched. "Semi-rigid" is not one of the three named categories.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation of this exact three-type taxonomy.

questions.prompt: What is the minimum volume required for a bailer carried in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft?

choices.text: At least 2 liters
At least 200 milliliters
choices.misconception: Understates the required volume by a factor of 10 -- far too small to bail meaningful water.

choices.text: At least 10 liters
choices.misconception: Overstates the requirement well beyond the regulatory minimum.

choices.text: No minimum volume is specified
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific 2 L minimum the regulation states.

hints: This is a small, specific figure in liters, not gallons.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(c) requires each bailer in a SOLAS inflatable liferaft to have a volume of at least 2 liters -- reproduced directly in the class-notes deck's CFR excerpt slide.
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- direct CFR citation, also reproduced verbatim in the class deck.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck's equipment-bag list, what is the liferaft's heaving line equipped with?

choices.text: A quoit
A signal whistle
choices.misconception: Confuses the heaving line's own fitting with a separate, independently-required whistle item.

choices.text: A strobe light
choices.misconception: Invents a fitting not listed for the heaving line.

choices.text: A folding grapnel hook
choices.misconception: Invents a fitting not listed for the heaving line.

hints: A quoit is a ring-shaped throwable float, commonly paired with a line meant to be thrown to someone in the water.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck's equipment-bag list specifically describes a "heaving line with quoit" -- the ring-shaped float attached to the line makes it easier to throw accurately and easier for a person in the water to grab and hold onto.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Not found in the CFR equipment-list text extracted so far (which names a heaving line but doesn't mention a quoit).

questions.prompt: What does a SOLAS inflatable liferaft's repair outfit require, at minimum?

choices.text: Six or more sealing clamps or serrated conical plugs (or a combination), and five or more tube patches at least 2 inches in diameter
Exactly one universal patch kit, with no minimum quantities specified
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement -- specific minimum counts and sizes are set for both clamps/plugs and patches.

choices.text: Two sealing clamps and two tube patches, of any size
choices.misconception: Understates both the required quantity and the minimum patch size.

choices.text: Ten sealing clamps and no patches at all
choices.misconception: Overstates the clamp count and omits the separately-required tube patches.

hints: Two different repair items each have their own minimum count -- one for punctures needing clamps/plugs, one for patches.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(y) requires a liferaft's repair outfit to include six or more sealing clamps or serrated conical plugs (or a combination of the two), and five or more tube patches at least 50 mm (2 in) in diameter, plus a roughing tool if needed to apply the patches.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 4 (CFR excerpt) and 7 (repair kit detail)
questions.sourceReference: 46 CFR 160.151-21(y), extracted directly from Document(6).PDF.pdf (public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-sea-trials/). Quantities also confirmed by the class deck's own repair-kit slide (slide 7: 6 sealing clamps for larger holes, 5 rubber patches for smaller ones).
questions.uncertaintyNote: The class deck's slide 7 splits the counts by hole size (5 patches for small holes ≤2in, 6 clamps for larger holes) slightly differently framed than the CFR's single combined repair-outfit requirement -- both give the same numbers, just organized differently. Not a conflict, just a framing difference worth noting if this question is refined further.

questions.prompt: What must a liferaft's pump or bellows be capable of?

choices.text: Being manually operated, and capable of inflating any part of the liferaft's inflatable structure
Running only on the liferaft's water-activated battery
choices.misconception: Contradicts the manual-operation requirement -- the pump/bellows is not a powered device.

choices.text: Inflating only the buoyancy tubes, not the floor
choices.misconception: Understates the requirement -- it must be able to inflate any part of the inflatable structure, including the floor.

choices.text: Automatically activating whenever the CO2 pressure drops
choices.misconception: Confuses the manually-operated backup pump with the automatic CO2 inflation system, which are two different things.

hints: This is the backup, hand-operated way to add air -- distinct from the automatic CO2 system.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(z) requires the pump or bellows to be manually operable and arranged so it's capable of inflating any part of the liferaft's inflatable structure -- the hand-operated backup to the automatic CO2 inflation system, useful for topping off buoyancy tubes on a cold night, for example.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 4 (CFR excerpt) and 12 ("you may need to pump up the buoyancy tubes with the hand pump")
questions.sourceReference: 46 CFR 160.151-21(z), extracted directly from Document(6).PDF.pdf (public/content/vinci/2026-07-08-sea-trials/). Practical use-case confirmed by the class deck's slide 12.
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- direct CFR citation, reinforced by the class deck's own practical description of when the hand pump is used.

questions.prompt: If a liferaft's pressure-relief valves are the type that need separate plugs to render them inoperable, what requirement applies to those plugs?

choices.text: They must be usable with hands gloved in an immersion suit, and must either float or be secured to the liferaft by a lanyard
They must be stored in a sealed, locked compartment inaccessible to occupants
choices.misconception: Invents a storage restriction that would defeat the purpose -- the plugs need to be usable by occupants, not locked away.

choices.text: They must be permanently fixed in place and never removed
choices.misconception: Contradicts their entire purpose -- they're meant to be applied by occupants when needed, not fixed.

choices.text: They require no special design consideration
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific glove-usability and float/lanyard requirements the regulation states.

hints: Think about who's applying these plugs, and under what conditions -- likely wearing bulky gloves, likely not wanting to lose a small part overboard.
questions.explanation: 46 CFR 160.151-21(aa) requires plugs for rendering pressure-relief valves inoperable (after they finish venting) to be usable with hands gloved in an immersion suit, and to either float or be secured to the liferaft by a lanyard -- both requirements account for realistic survivor conditions.
questions.uncertaintyNote: None -- direct CFR citation.

questions.prompt: After patching a small hole (2 inches or less) in a liferaft, how long must you wait before "topping off" (fully re-inflating) the raft?

choices.text: 24 hours
Topping off should happen immediately, to restore full buoyancy right away
choices.misconception: This is exactly the instinct the wait period exists to override -- the patch needs time to set before it's tested with full pressure.

choices.text: 1 hour
There is no wait period -- top off as soon as the patch is visibly dry
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific 24-hour figure and substitutes an unreliable visual judgment instead.

hints: The instinctive move -- restore full pressure right away -- is the wrong one here.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states that after using the repair kit to patch a small hole (2 inches or less, using rubber patches, roughing tool, and cement), you must wait 24 hours before "topping off" the liferaft -- giving the patch time to properly set before it's put under full inflation pressure.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck's labeled equipment photo, how many of each pyrotechnic/smoke signal does a liferaft carry?

choices.text: 4 parachute rocket red flares, 6 hand-held red flares, and 2 orange smoke signals
2 parachute rocket red flares, 2 hand-held red flares, and 2 orange smoke signals
choices.misconception: Understates both flare counts -- the deck's labeled photo shows 4 parachute and 6 hand-held flares, not 2 of each.

choices.text: 6 parachute rocket red flares, 4 hand-held red flares, and 2 orange smoke signals
choices.misconception: Swaps the parachute and hand-held flare counts -- it's 4 parachute and 6 hand-held, not the reverse.

choices.text: 4 parachute rocket red flares, 6 hand-held red flares, and no smoke signals
choices.misconception: Omits the 2 orange smoke signals the deck's photo clearly labels as part of the kit.

hints: Two of the three quantities match each other by symmetry with a different figure; the third stands alone.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck's labeled equipment photo shows exactly 4 parachute rocket red flares, 6 hand-held red flares, and 2 orange smoke signals as part of a liferaft's signaling kit.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only -- these are specific product-kit quantities, not quantities mandated by the CFR equipment-list text extracted so far (which requires the devices but doesn't specify counts in what's been extracted). Awaiting manual or further CFR/IMO confirmation.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, what is the total water capacity a liferaft is able to carry per person -- as distinct from the daily drinking ration once rationing begins?

choices.text: 1.5 liters total per person
1 pint per person, with no separate total-capacity figure
choices.misconception: Confuses the daily rationing rate (1 pint/person/day, once rationing begins) with the total stowed capacity, which is a different, larger figure.

choices.text: 5 liters total per person
choices.misconception: Overstates the total capacity figure the class deck gives.

choices.text: 3 liters total per person
choices.misconception: This is the already-live lifeboat stowage figure from a different regulation -- not the liferaft's total capacity figure given in this deck.

hints: This is a total stowage capacity, not a daily ration rate -- a different kind of figure from the 1-pint-per-day rule.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states a liferaft is able to carry 1.5 liters total per person -- a total stowage capacity, distinct from the 1-pint-per-day rationing rate that applies once rationing begins (the same rate already confirmed elsewhere in this course for general survival craft).
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Worth comparing carefully in a future Study Guide to the already-live lifeboat water-stowage figure (3 liters per person, q-ch4-waterrequirement-official) -- a different figure for a different craft, not a conflict, but a real point of possible confusion.

questions.prompt: In order, what holds a liferaft container in its cradle, and what releases it for launching?

choices.text: Packing bands hold the container's shape; a tie-down strap secures the container to the cradle; a hydrostatic release (manual or automatic) releases the tie-down strap
The hydrostatic release holds the container's shape, and the tie-down strap is only used after the raft has already inflated
choices.misconception: Reverses the roles -- the hydrostatic release is what triggers release of the tie-down strap, not something used only after inflation.

choices.text: Packing bands alone secure the container to the cradle, with no separate tie-down strap or release mechanism
choices.misconception: Omits the separate tie-down strap and hydrostatic release, which do the actual securing-to-cradle and releasing.

choices.text: A cleat on deck is what secures the container to the cradle
choices.misconception: Confuses the deck cleat (used to secure the sea painter/operating cord during manual launching) with the tie-down strap that actually secures the container to the cradle.

hints: Three different pieces of hardware do three different jobs here -- don't conflate them.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck describes the stowage arrangement in sequence: packing bands hold the container together, a tie-down strap secures the container to its cradle, and a hydrostatic release (tripped manually via a pelican hook, or automatically underwater) releases the tie-down strap. A separate deck cleat is used only to secure the sea painter/operating cord during a manual launch -- a different piece of hardware entirely.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: During automatic launching, what are the two ends of the painter/operating cord attached to?

choices.text: One end is attached by the weak link to the cradle; the other end is attached to the raft's inflation tank and the towing bridle
Both ends are attached to the cradle, forming a closed loop
choices.misconception: Contradicts the actual arrangement -- only one end attaches to the cradle; the other attaches to the raft itself.

choices.text: One end is attached to the ship's hull directly, with no connection to the raft's inflation system at all
choices.misconception: Omits the operating cord's actual role in triggering inflation via the raft's inflation tank.

choices.text: Neither end is attached to anything until a crew member manually connects them during launch
choices.misconception: Contradicts the automatic-launch design, which requires both ends to already be connected for the sequence to work without crew action.

hints: One end anchors to the ship (via a deliberately weak connection); the other end does two jobs on the raft side.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck describes the painter/operating cord's two ends: one end is attached by the weak link to the cradle, and the other end is attached to the raft's inflation tank and the towing bridle. As the ship sinks, the cord pays out, pulling the inflation-tank end taut enough to activate inflation -- and once the inflated raft's buoyancy exceeds the weak link's strength, that link breaks, freeing the raft from the sinking ship.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only, though conceptually consistent with 46 CFR 160.151-17(l)(2)'s painter/inflation-cylinder link requirement found in the prior readiness audit (that CFR text confirms the general linkage concept but not this specific two-end wiring description).

questions.prompt: Why does the class-notes deck specifically caution survivors to "watch for leaks" regarding a liferaft's CO2 inflation gas?

choices.text: Because CO2 is colorless and odorless, so a leak wouldn't be detected by sight or smell
Because CO2 is flammable and could ignite from a spark
choices.misconception: CO2 is not flammable -- the caution is about detectability, not fire risk.

choices.text: Because CO2 leaks would cause the raft to overinflate and burst
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual concern -- a leak means gas is escaping (under-inflation risk), not building up excess pressure.

choices.text: Because CO2 leaks are loud and could attract unwanted attention
choices.misconception: Invents a concern not stated in the source, and actually reverses the real issue -- CO2 is undetectable, not noisy.

hints: The caution is about your senses failing to catch the problem, not about danger from the gas itself.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck notes the CO2 used to inflate the raft is colorless and odorless, so survivors must watch for leaks rather than relying on smell or sight -- distinct from the normal, expected gas venting that happens right after inflation via the pressure relief valves.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: Why might a liferaft's buoyancy tubes need attention at both midday and at night?

choices.text: During the day, rising temperature expands the CO2 and the pressure relief valve may bleed off excess pressure; at night, dropping temperature may require pumping the tubes back up with the hand pump
The pressure relief valve only functions at night, so daytime pressure must be managed manually
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual mechanism -- the PRV manages excess daytime pressure automatically; the hand pump is the manual tool used at night.

choices.text: Buoyancy tubes need no attention at any time once initially inflated
choices.misconception: Contradicts the described day/night pressure cycle entirely.

choices.text: The hand pump is used during the day to add extra pressure beyond the CO2 system's capacity
choices.misconception: Reverses when the hand pump is actually needed -- it's for the nighttime pressure drop, not added daytime pressure.

hints: Gas expands when warmed and contracts when cooled -- think about what that does to the tubes across a full day.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck explains a daily pressure cycle: during the day, rising temperature expands the CO2 inside the buoyancy tubes, and the pressure relief valve may bleed off the excess; at night, dropping temperature contracts the gas, and the tubes may need to be pumped back up by hand.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: A liferaft's buoyancy tubes are divided into two separate compartments. What is the safety benefit of this design?

choices.text: The raft will hold its rated number of persons even if only half the compartments remain inflated
It allows the raft to be inflated twice as fast, since two compartments fill in parallel
choices.misconception: Misidentifies the purpose -- the two-compartment design is about damage redundancy, not inflation speed.

choices.text: It doubles the raft's rated capacity compared to a single-compartment design
choices.misconception: Overstates the benefit -- the redundancy preserves the rated capacity if one compartment is damaged, it doesn't double it.

choices.text: It allows one compartment to be deliberately deflated for storage without affecting the other
choices.misconception: Describes a storage convenience, not the actual stated safety purpose (surviving damage to one compartment while afloat).

hints: Think about what happens if one compartment is punctured or damaged while the raft is in use.
questions.explanation: A liferaft's buoyancy tubes (thick, nylon-reinforced rubber, located on the outer edge) are divided into two separate compartments specifically so that damage to one won't sink the raft -- it will still hold its rated number of persons even if only half the compartments remain inflated.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Conceptually consistent with (but not the same specific fact as) the CFR stability-appendage redundancy requirements found in the prior readiness audit (46 CFR 160.151-1(a)(2)(iv), which requires stability appendages to consist of at least two separate parts for the same damage-redundancy reason).

questions.prompt: Beyond providing insulation, what additional use does some liferafts' removable floor have?

choices.text: It can be removed and used as an extra float
It can be removed and used as an emergency paddle
choices.misconception: Invents a use not stated in the source -- the floor's stated secondary use is as a float, not a paddle.

choices.text: It can be removed and used to patch a hole in the buoyancy tubes
choices.misconception: Confuses the floor with the separately-carried repair patches meant for exactly that purpose.

choices.text: It cannot be removed under any circumstances once installed
choices.misconception: Contradicts the class-notes deck's statement that some floors can be removed.

hints: Think about what an inflatable, buoyant panel could be repurposed as once separated from the raft.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck notes that some liferaft floors can be removed and used as an extra float -- in addition to the floor's primary role of providing cold-water insulation when inflated (or cooling when deflated in hot weather).
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation. Complements, without duplicating, the already-live q-quizr1-floor-vs-canopy and q-quiz6-liferaft-climate-adaptation-compare-vh, which test the floor's insulation function, not this secondary extra-float use.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, which of the following is NOT one of the three ways to launch a liferaft?

choices.text: Free-fall
Davit launched
Manual (thrown over the side, then inflated)
Automatic (triggered as the ship sinks)

hints: Free-fall launching is a real lifeboat launching method taught elsewhere in this course -- but it's not one of the three liferaft methods this deck lists.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck lists exactly three ways to launch a liferaft: davit launched (preferred), manual (thrown over the side and then inflated), and automatic (as the ship sinks). "Free-fall" launching is a lifeboat launching method, not a liferaft one, in this source.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Deliberately uses "free-fall" as the wrong answer since it's a real lifeboat concept from Chapter 2 -- worth reviewing this distractor specifically for cross-chapter confusion risk before promotion.

questions.prompt: The class-notes deck ranks all three liferaft launching methods by preference. What is the full ranking, and the stated reason for each?

choices.text: Davit (most preferred -- you board dry), then Manual (next preferred -- you control the launch), then Automatic (least preferred -- it only happens as the ship is sinking)
Automatic (most preferred, since it requires no crew action), then Manual, then Davit (least preferred, since it requires special equipment)
choices.misconception: Inverts the actual ranking -- automatic launching is explicitly the least preferred, since it only occurs as the ship is already sinking.

choices.text: Manual (most preferred, since crew always control it), then Davit, then Automatic
choices.misconception: Misorders davit and manual -- davit is the most preferred, specifically because it lets you board dry.

choices.text: All three methods are considered equally preferable, with no stated ranking
choices.misconception: Contradicts the class-notes deck's explicit preference ranking and reasoning for each method.

hints: The reasoning for each method's rank matters as much as the order itself: staying dry, staying in control, and running out of options are three different situations.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck ranks all three methods: davit launching is most preferred because you board the raft dry; manual launching is next preferred because the crew controls the timing; automatic launching is least preferred because, by definition, it only happens as the ship is sinking and no one has manually launched yet.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. This tests the full three-way ranking and reasoning; it does not duplicate the already-live q-quiz1a-preferred-liferaft-launch, which only tests that davit is preferred, without the reasoning or the full ranking of all three.

questions.prompt: SCENARIO: A liferaft has inflated and is floating next to the sinking ship. All survivors have now boarded. What should be done with the painter/operating cord?

choices.text: Cut the painter -- its job of holding the raft alongside until everyone boarded is now complete
Leave the painter attached indefinitely, since it will be needed again later
choices.misconception: Contradicts the described procedure -- the painter is specifically cut once boarding is complete, to separate the raft from the sinking ship.

choices.text: Use the painter to tow the raft closer to the ship for additional supplies
choices.misconception: Contradicts the goal at this stage, which is separating from the sinking ship, not moving closer to it.

choices.text: Deflate the raft partially before cutting the painter
choices.misconception: Invents an unnecessary and counterproductive step -- there's no reason to deflate a raft you're trying to keep afloat and clear of a sinking ship.

hints: The painter's job during this phase is temporary -- it exists to hold the raft in place only until it's no longer needed.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck describes the operating cord (painter) as acting as a sea painter to hold the raft alongside the ship until it is boarded -- and once all persons are in the raft, the painter is cut, freeing the raft to drift clear of the sinking vessel.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: SCENARIO: A rigid liferaft is stowed 36 meters (about 118 feet) above the light waterline. Using the class-notes deck's painter-length rule, what is the minimum painter length required?

choices.text: 46 meters -- 10 meters plus the 36-meter stowage height, since that total is greater than the 15-meter alternative minimum
15 meters flat, regardless of stowage height
choices.misconception: Applies only the fallback minimum -- but the rule is 10m plus stowage height OR 15m, whichever is GREATER, and 46m is far greater than 15m here.

choices.text: 36 meters, matching the stowage height exactly with no additional margin
choices.misconception: Omits the required 10-meter addition to the stowage height.

choices.text: 10 meters flat, regardless of stowage height
choices.misconception: Applies only the base figure without adding the stowage-height component the rule requires.

hints: The rule has two components added together, compared against a flat fallback minimum -- use whichever result is larger.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states a rigid liferaft's painter must be at least 10 meters plus the distance from the stowed position to the light waterline, or 15 meters, whichever is greater. At a 36-meter stowage height, 10 + 36 = 46 meters, which is far greater than the 15-meter fallback -- so 46 meters is the required minimum. (The deck's own worked example uses a 50-meter painter at a 36-meter stowage height, an actual length safely above this 46-meter minimum.)
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. This is a distinct fact from the flat "100 ft operating cord" figure tested in q-ch3cn-operating-cord-length-corrected -- the class deck presents both a formula (for a rigid liferaft's painter) and a flat figure (for "the operating cord") without fully reconciling whether these describe the same line under different conditions or two different specifications; flagged here, not resolved.

questions.prompt: Why does the class-notes deck specifically warn against jumping onto the top of a liferaft's canopy arch?

choices.text: It can injure people already inside the raft, and equipment stowed in the raft may injure the jumper too
The canopy is electrically charged from the water-activated battery
choices.misconception: Invents a hazard not stated in the source -- the actual concern is physical injury from impact and stowed equipment, not electrical.

choices.text: It would trigger the CO2 inflation system a second time
choices.misconception: Invents a mechanical consequence not described in the source.

choices.text: It voids the raft's USCG approval
choices.misconception: Invents a regulatory consequence not stated anywhere in this source.

hints: Think about who's already inside, and what else might already be inside, when someone lands on top.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck warns not to jump onto the top of the canopy arch: doing so risks injuring people already inside the raft, and equipment stowed in the raft may injure the jumper as well -- part of why jumping into the canopy opening is separately marked "NOT RECOMMENDED" as a boarding method.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Complements, without duplicating, the already-drafted q-ch3cn-boarding-methods-list (which identifies WHICH method is discouraged; this question tests WHY).

questions.prompt: A capsized liferaft has a specific side marked to indicate where a survivor should swim to right it. What does that marking say, and which side is it on?

choices.text: "Right Here" -- marked on the side where the CO2 cylinder is located
"Pull Here" -- marked on the side opposite the CO2 cylinder
choices.misconception: Both the wording and the side are wrong -- the actual marking reads "Right Here" and is on the CO2-cylinder side, not the opposite side.

choices.text: "Righting Strap" -- marked at the entrance, not on the underside
choices.misconception: Confuses the marking's wording and location -- the underside marking reads "Right Here," not the equipment's own label.

choices.text: No specific marking exists -- survivors must locate the CO2 cylinder by feel
choices.misconception: Contradicts the class deck's description of a specific, readable marking.

hints: The wording is a play on words -- it tells you both what to do and, phonetically, hints at the side to use.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states a capsized liferaft has its underside marked "Right Here" on the side where the CO2 cylinder is located -- the same side already confirmed by this course's official-source-supported righting-technique questions, now with the specific marking wording added.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only for the specific marking wording. The underlying CO2-bottle-side fact is already official-source-supported via the live q-raft-2/q-raft-11.

questions.prompt: What is the correct sequence and technique for righting a capsized liferaft once you've reached the correct side?

choices.text: Reach up and grab the righting strap, positioning yourself so the wind blows toward you, lean back and pull, and continue pulling -- pulling yourself free of the raft -- without letting go
Grab the righting strap and push forward, away from the raft, to create separation before pulling
choices.misconception: Reverses the technique -- you lean back and pull, using your body weight, not push away.

choices.text: Position yourself so the wind blows away from you, to avoid being pushed into the raft
choices.misconception: Reverses the wind guidance -- the wind should be blowing toward you as you right the raft.

choices.text: Grab the strap, pull once, and immediately let go to avoid being pulled under as the raft flips
choices.misconception: Contradicts the deck's explicit, emphasized instruction: continue pulling and do NOT let go.

hints: Wind direction, body position, and grip all matter together -- and one instruction in the source is emphasized with capital letters and exclamation points.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck's righting procedure: swim to the "Right Here" (CO2-cylinder) side with the wind blowing toward you, reach up and grab the righting strap, lean back and pull, and continue pulling -- pulling yourself free of the raft as it comes over. The deck emphasizes this last point strongly: "DO NOT LET GO!!!"
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only for the wind-direction and don't-let-go details; the core CO2-bottle-side/strap-pull technique is already official-source-supported via live questions.

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, is righting a capsized liferaft always a one-person task?

choices.text: No -- some rafts require two people to use the righting strap and right the raft
Yes, without exception, one person is always sufficient
choices.misconception: Contradicts the class deck's specific statement that some rafts need two people.

choices.text: No -- righting always requires at least three people
choices.misconception: Overstates the requirement -- the deck specifies two people for the rafts that need help, not three.

choices.text: The deck doesn't address how many people are needed
choices.misconception: The deck does address this directly.

hints: The answer depends on the specific raft -- this isn't a universal one-person-only or two-person-only rule.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck notes that some liferafts will require two people to use the righting strap and right the raft -- righting capability can vary by raft size and design, so a lone survivor shouldn't assume success is guaranteed without help.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation.

questions.prompt: SCENARIO: While attempting to right a capsized liferaft, it falls over on top of you. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain contact with the raft, stay calm -- it will not harm you -- and use the righting strap to work your way out from underneath
Immediately swim away from the raft entirely and attempt to right it again later from a distance
choices.misconception: Losing contact with the raft in open water is a worse position than staying with it, and the deck specifically describes working your way out while maintaining contact, not abandoning the attempt.

choices.text: Hold your breath and wait for someone else to free you, since a survivor cannot free themselves in this position
choices.misconception: Contradicts the deck's guidance, which describes exactly how a survivor can and should self-extract using the righting strap.

choices.text: Push straight up against the raft's underside with both hands until it lifts off
choices.misconception: Not the described technique -- the deck specifically directs using the righting strap to work free, not pushing directly against the raft.

hints: This scenario addresses a real fear -- but the deck's guidance is reassuring about what's actually dangerous here, and what isn't.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck directly addresses this scenario: if the raft falls over on top of you while righting it, maintain contact with the raft at all times -- it will fall over on top of you but will not harm you -- and use the righting strap to work your way out from underneath.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Directly addresses a likely student misconception (that being trapped under the raft is itself dangerous), making this a strong misconception-correcting candidate even before manual confirmation exists.

questions.prompt: What principle governs where a liferaft's sea anchor is attached?

choices.text: It must be attached so the primary entrance is oriented away from the seas as far as practicable, while still letting a person inside the raft retrieve it
It must be attached so the primary entrance faces directly into the seas, for maximum stability
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual orientation goal -- the entrance should face AWAY from the seas, not into them.

choices.text: Attachment position doesn't matter, as long as the anchor itself is heavy enough
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific orientation principle the source describes -- position matters as much as the anchor itself.

choices.text: It must be attached so it can only be retrieved from outside the raft, never from inside
choices.misconception: Reverses the actual requirement -- the anchor must remain retrievable BY a person INSIDE the raft.

hints: Two goals have to be balanced at once: protecting the entrance from waves, and keeping the anchor within reach.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states sea anchors must be attached to the raft at a position that orients the primary entrance away from the seas as far as practicable, while still allowing the sea anchor to be retrieved by a person inside the raft -- balancing wave protection against practical access.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Awaiting manual or CFR confirmation (the CFR sea-anchor subsection extracted so far, 160.151-21(e), covers towing-related detail but not this specific entrance-orientation principle).

questions.prompt: According to the class-notes deck, at what speed can a liferaft be towed in calm water without capsizing?

choices.text: 3 knots
10 knots
choices.misconception: Overstates the safe towing speed well beyond what the class deck specifies.

choices.text: 1 knot
choices.misconception: Understates the figure -- the deck states a higher safe towing speed than this.

choices.text: No towing speed limit is stated
choices.misconception: Contradicts the specific 3-knot figure the class deck gives.

hints: This is a low, cautious speed -- liferafts are far less stable under tow than a lifeboat.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states a liferaft is able to be towed at a speed of 3 knots in calm water without capsizing -- a low figure reflecting a liferaft's large sail area, shallow draft, and shape, all of which make it far less stable under tow than a rigid lifeboat.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. Worth comparing in a future Study Guide to the already-live lifeboat/rescue-boat towing speeds taught elsewhere in this course (2 knots towing a 25-person liferaft, 6 knots unloaded) -- those describe a rescue boat's towing capability, not a liferaft's own tow tolerance, so not a conflict, but a related figure worth cross-referencing.

questions.prompt: Why does the class-notes deck describe liferafts as harder to handle than lifeboats?

choices.text: Rafts are less maneuverable due to their large sail area, shallow draft, and shape -- a high wind could easily capsize one if the crew doesn't rig a sea anchor
Liferafts have no equipment for maneuvering at all, unlike lifeboats
choices.misconception: Contradicts the deck's own equipment list, which specifically names a sea anchor and paddles as liferaft maneuvering equipment.

choices.text: Liferafts are heavier than lifeboats, making them slower to respond to steering input
choices.misconception: Misidentifies the reason -- the deck attributes the handling difficulty to sail area, draft, and shape, not weight.

choices.text: Liferafts require a licensed operator, while lifeboats do not
choices.misconception: Invents a licensing distinction not stated in this source, and not the reason given for the handling difference.

hints: Think about a liferaft's shape and how much of it sits above versus below the waterline, compared to a lifeboat's hull.
questions.explanation: The class-notes deck states it is much harder to handle liferafts than lifeboats: rafts are less maneuverable due to their large sail area, shallow draft, and shape, and a high wind could easily capsize a raft if the crew doesn't rig a sea anchor to stabilize it.
questions.uncertaintyNote: Class-note-supported only. This is a direct liferaft-vs-lifeboat comparison -- checked against existing Chapter 2 lifeboat-handling content and found to be complementary, not conflicting (Chapter 2 doesn't make a comparative claim about liferafts, so there's no cross-chapter tension here).

questions.prompt: On the class-notes deck's labeled liferaft parts diagram, what is the CO2 tripping lanyard connected between?

choices.text: The towing bridle and the operating cord (painter) -- it's the short connector positioned between them, distinct from the long coiled painter itself
The canopy and the recognition light
choices.misconception: Misplaces the CO2 tripping lanyard -- on the diagram, it sits between the towing bridle and the operating cord, not near the canopy or lights.

choices.text: The buoyancy tubes and the boarding ladder
choices.misconception: Misplaces the CO2 tripping lanyard -- it isn't positioned near the buoyancy tubes or boarding ladder on the diagram.

choices.text: The sea anchor and the weak link, with no connection to the towing bridle at all
choices.misconception: Confuses the CO2 tripping lanyard's position with the sea-anchor line, which is a separate labeled item entirely on the diagram.

hints: Follow the diagram's line from the raft's underside downward: towing bridle, then this short connector, then the long coiled cord.
questions.explanation: On the class-notes deck's labeled liferaft parts diagram, the CO2 tripping lanyard is positioned between the towing bridle and the operating cord (painter) -- the short connecting segment that, when the painter is pulled taut as the ship sinks, trips the CO2 inflation system.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 22 and 36 (identical diagram)
questions.uncertaintyNote: DIAGRAM-UNCERTAIN: the underlying diagram content is clear and fully labeled (not ambiguous), but this question needs a cropped, embedded image of the diagram prepared before it can be promoted as a proper visual_study question matching this course's existing diagram-question pattern (image + imageAlt fields). Held pending that asset-preparation step, not pending source clarification.

questions.prompt: On the class-notes deck's labeled liferaft parts diagram, where are the equipment bags and the rain water catchment tube positioned relative to each other?

choices.text: Both are labeled near the top of the canopy, close to the recognition light and heaving line
The equipment bags are on the buoyancy tubes at the raft's base, while the rain water catchment tube is on the underside near the sea anchor
choices.misconception: Misplaces both items -- on the diagram, both are labeled near the top of the canopy, not at the raft's base or underside.

choices.text: The rain water catchment tube is inside the canopy interior, and the equipment bags are external, hanging below the buoyancy tubes
choices.misconception: Misplaces both items relative to their actual labeled positions near the canopy top.

choices.text: They are not both shown on the same diagram
choices.misconception: Contradicts the diagram, which labels both items.

hints: Both labels point to the upper portion of the raft, not the underside where the painter/sea-anchor rigging is shown.
questions.explanation: On the class-notes deck's labeled liferaft parts diagram, both the equipment bags and the rain water catchment tube are labeled near the top of the canopy, alongside the recognition light and the heaving line -- all upper-canopy features, distinct from the underside rigging (weak link, painter, sea anchor, towing bridle) shown lower on the same diagram.
questions.slideReference: Chapter 3 class-notes deck, slides 22 and 36 (identical diagram)
questions.uncertaintyNote: DIAGRAM-UNCERTAIN: same asset-preparation status as q-ch3cn-diagram-co2-tripping-lanyard -- the diagram content itself is clear, but a cropped, embedded image is needed before this can be promoted as a proper visual_study question.
